name: LEVER Capability Framework
description: LEVER (Learn, Execute, Value, Enable, Replicate) is a capability multiplication framework that synthesizes classical learning models (Bloom's Taxonomy, Dreyfus Model, Shu-Ha-Ri, Apprenticeship) for the AI era. It describes how individuals progress from acquiring knowledge to creating value, enabling others, and ultimately replicating capability through systems, automation, AI agents, platforms, and institutions.

stages.name: Learn
stages.question: What do I know?
stages.description: Acquire knowledge through study, observation, and instruction. Build vocabulary, concepts, and mental models. This is the foundation of all capability development.

stages.name: Execute
stages.question: What can I do?
stages.description: Apply knowledge to produce work. Move from theory to practice. Requires guidance and supervision but can complete defined tasks.

stages.name: Value
stages.question: Can I independently create outcomes?
stages.description: Demonstrate independent ability to create value. Operate without supervision. Make decisions and own results. This is where practitioners become trusted contributors.

stages.name: Enable
stages.question: Can I make others successful?
stages.description: Develop other people's capabilities. Mentor, coach, teach, and lead. Output shifts from personal work to growing others. This is where practitioners become leaders.

stages.name: Replicate
stages.question: Can I make success repeatable at scale?
stages.description: Create systems that multiply capability beyond direct human interaction. Build platforms, standards, frameworks, AI agents, automation, and institutions that enable many others to succeed. This is the AI-era extension of classical mastery models.

frameworks.name: Bloom's Taxonomy
frameworks.description: A hierarchical model of cognitive learning objectives widely used in education.

stages.name: Remember
stages.description: Recall facts and basic concepts

stages.name: Understand
stages.description: Explain ideas or concepts

stages.name: Apply
stages.description: Use information in new situations

stages.name: Analyze
stages.description: Draw connections among ideas

stages.name: Evaluate
stages.description: Justify a decision or course of action

stages.name: Create
stages.description: Produce new or original work

frameworks.name: Dreyfus Model of Skill Acquisition
frameworks.description: A model describing how students acquire skills through formal instruction and practice.

stages.name: Novice
stages.description: Follows rules exactly, requires explicit instructions

stages.name: Advanced Beginner
stages.description: Recognizes patterns from experience

stages.name: Competent
stages.description: Plans and prioritizes, manages complexity

stages.name: Proficient
stages.description: Sees situations holistically, knows what matters

stages.name: Expert
stages.description: Operates intuitively, transcends rules

frameworks.name: Shu-Ha-Ri
frameworks.description: A Japanese martial arts concept describing stages of learning to mastery.
frameworks.origin: Japanese martial arts tradition, popularized by Alistair Cockburn in software

stages.name: Shu (守)
stages.description: Obey - Follow the rules exactly, learn the forms

stages.name: Ha (破)
stages.description: Detach - Break from tradition, understand when rules bend

stages.name: Ri (離)
stages.description: Transcend - Create your own approach, rules become instinct

frameworks.name: Apprenticeship Model
frameworks.description: The traditional craft-based progression from apprentice to master.
frameworks.origin: Medieval guild system, formalized craft traditions

stages.name: Observe
stages.description: Watch experts perform the craft

stages.name: Assist
stages.description: Help experts with supervised tasks

stages.name: Perform
stages.description: Work independently on full tasks

stages.name: Lead
stages.description: Guide others, take responsibility for outcomes

stages.name: Teach
stages.description: Train apprentices, preserve and advance the craft

frameworks.name: Conscious Competence Model
frameworks.description: A model describing the psychological states in learning a new skill.

stages.name: Unconscious Incompetence
stages.description: Don't know what you don't know

stages.name: Conscious Incompetence
stages.description: Aware of skill gap, learning begins

stages.name: Conscious Competence
stages.description: Can perform with deliberate effort

stages.name: Unconscious Competence
stages.description: Skill becomes automatic

mappings.notes: Knowledge acquisition covers basic recall and comprehension
Execution is the application of learned knowledge
Creating value requires analysis and judgment
Enabling others often requires creating new approaches
Beyond Bloom - multiplication through systems is not addressed
Novices are learning rules and fundamentals
Execution spans pattern recognition to planning
Value creation requires holistic understanding and intuition
Not directly addressed - often called 'Master' in extensions
Beyond Dreyfus - some extensions add 'Teacher' level
Shu is about learning and following the forms
Still within Shu - applying the learned forms
Ha - breaking from tradition, understanding context
Ri practitioners often become teachers
Advanced Ri - creating new schools and approaches
Observation is the primary learning mechanism
Execution spans assisted to independent work
Full independent performance with proven results
Leading and teaching others
Master teachers who create schools and guilds
Learning begins with awareness of the gap
Deliberate, effortful performance
Automatic, reliable performance
Often called 'Reflective Mastery' in extensions
Beyond the model - requires conscious articulation

reference_profiles.name: Product Builder
reference_profiles.description: LEVER interpretation for Product Builders - individuals who own end-to-end product outcomes.

stage_interpretations.title: Learner
stage_interpretations.description: Acquiring product, technology, and business knowledge.
evidence: Studies successful products and case studies
examples: Learns to use AI coding assistants

stage_interpretations.title: Practitioner
stage_interpretations.description: Building prototypes and MVPs with guidance.
evidence: Uses AI tools to accelerate development
examples: Builds an internal tool with AI assistance

stage_interpretations.title: Builder
stage_interpretations.description: Independently delivering products that create business value.
examples: Launches a product that acquires users
Improves key metrics through product changes

stage_interpretations.title: Leader
examples: Mentors a practitioner to builder level

stage_interpretations.title: Multiplier
stage_interpretations.description: Creating systems that scale product capability.
examples: Creates a product requirements AI agent
Writes a product playbook adopted by teams
Establishes a product guild or community
Builds a platform used by other builders

reference_profiles.name: Software Engineer
reference_profiles.description: LEVER interpretation for software engineers building production systems.

stage_interpretations.title: Engineer
stage_interpretations.description: Implementing features and fixing bugs with guidance.

stage_interpretations.title: Staff Engineer
stage_interpretations.description: Elevating team capability and technical direction.

stage_interpretations.title: Principal/Architect
stage_interpretations.description: Creating systems that multiply engineering capability.
